hypothesis: Cron scheduler refinement through gradient-driven scheduling — instead of fixed-interval cron cycles, use gradient magnitude and direction stability to dynamically adjust cycle frequency. When gradients are stable and low-magnitude, extend intervals (reducing overhead). When gradients spike or show directional shifts, compress intervals (increasing responsiveness). This creates an adaptive evolution rhythm that matches system state.
research_question: Can gradient-driven dynamic scheduling improve evolution responsiveness while reducing unnecessary cycle overhead?
expected_outcome: Establish a gradient-driven scheduling protocol that reduces unnecessary cron cycles by 40% while improving evolution responsiveness during gradient spikes
